observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to create an inventory committee to conduct the physical count of all PPE and submit the RPCPPE, in conformity with the provisions stipulated in COA Circular No. 2020-006.
We further recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to appoint a General Services Officer who will take charge in the monitoring of issuances and transfers of PPE and to prepare property cards. Likewise, a reconciliation of the actual physical count of property with the accounting records, and PPELCs with theTrial Balance be undertaken to ensure the reliability of PPE accounts as presented in the financial statements.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-01 dated March 11, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the appropriate adjusting journal entries to correct the misstatements of the affected accounts. Henceforth, ensure that costs of major repairs, rehabilitation and construction of infrastructure assets should be capitalized or recognized as asset in accordance with IPSAS No. 17.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-09 dated April 11, 2022

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Reconcile with the depository bank for the discrepancies noted between the bank statements and the agency records; and
Prepare the Journal Entry Voucher to effect the adjustments of the valid reconciling items appearing in the BRS in order to reflect the correct balance of the cash in bank account.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-15 dated April 27, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Bids and Awards Committee and other concerned officials to:
Familiarize themselves with the pertinent provisions prescribed by R.A. No. 9184 and its Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations and ensure that all the procurements made by the Municipality be in accordance with the same;
Indicate in the Purchase Orders the complete detailed specifications of the items procured in compliance with COA Circular No. 96-010; and
Henceforth, check and verify the Purchase Request (PR) whether the items requested have reference to brand names, if so, return the PR to the Office concerned for correction and revision.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum Nos. 2022-03 and 2022-08 dated March 11, 2022 and April 8, 2022, respectively.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and all officials concerned to ensure that in the implementation of infrastructure projects by administration, it should be covered and supported with duly approved plans and specifications.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-05 dated April 7, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Bids and Awards Committee to:
a.	Strictly comply with the provisions of Sections 48 and 52 of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA No. 9184. The alternative mode of procurement for shopping be adopted only whenever justified by the conditions provided by the procurement law; and
b.	Ensure that documentary requirements in the RA No. 9184 under the alternative mode of procurement are complied with by the suppliers. They should ensure that those suppliers with PhilGEPS Platinum Membership are not expired.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2020-04 dated April 7, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the concerned officials and employees to:
a.	Exert extra care in the post-qualification of the bids in order to properly determine whether the bidder concerned are responsive to all the requirements and conditions specified in the RA No. 9184; and
b.	Submit the lacking documents in order to further validate the propriety of the disbursements. Otherwise, the members of the Bids and Awards Committee and TWG submit a justification why the projects were awarded to the winning suppliers/contractors in spite non-submission of the documentary requirements of RA No. 9184.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-06 dated April 7, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Implement the prompt and proper liquidation of trust funds received from Source Agencies, including the return of excess/unutilized funds; and
Reconcile the unliquidated fund transfers with the Source Agencies and prepare the adjusting entries for the reconciling items noted.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-13 dated April 19, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct concerned officials and employees to closely monitor the implementation of projects/programs/activities for any fund received in order to maximize its utilization.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 20202-02 dated March 11, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the LDRRMO to ensure optimum utilization of the LDRRMF through proper planning, prioritization and fast-tracking the implementation of priority projects, programs and activities to afford its constituents the readiness of resources in times of disaster.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-14 dated April 20, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to henceforward recognize as inventory the supplies and materials for consumption or distribution upon receipt and subsequent issuance of the same should be supported with Requisition and Issuance Slip (RIS) in order to properly account for the inventories of the Municipality.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-10 dated April 11, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to process disbursement vouchers / claim on the advance payment / mobilization fee only upon submission to and acceptance of an irrevocable standby letter of credit, bank guarantee or a surety bond as prescribed under Section 4.2 of Annex E of Revised IRR of RA 9184.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-11 dated April 13, 2022.

We recommended and Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Bids and Awards Committee to strictly comply with R.A. No. 9184 and its Revised IRR on the timely posting of notices to achieve the objectives of promoting competitiveness and transparency in the procurement.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management through Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-12 dated April 18, 2022.

We recommended that Management stop the paying Transportation Allowance (TA) to officials with assigned government motor vehicles pursuant to Item 8.2.1 of DBM Local Budget Circular No. 103 dated May 15, 2013. Further, it is required that the amount of ₱14,400.00 and ₱13,600.00 or a total of ₱28,000.00 representing TA granted to the Municipal Mayor and Municipal Vice Mayor, respectively from November 2019 to December 2019 be refunded without prejudice to the same allowance granted for the months of January 2020 and succeeding period, if any.
observations.managementAction: A reply was made that though these vehicles are issued to the Office of the Mayor and Vice Mayor, these vehicles are used by municipal employees with official travels.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management in coordination with the Provincial Environmental Monitoring Board of Isabela and concerned barangay officials to:

Monitor quarry operations in their respectively barangays/localities;

Report to the concerned authorities (Provincial Management Monitoring Board) all quarry operators who are extracting quarry materials beyond the volume stated in the hauling slip and outside of the allowed quarry sites; and

Require the municipal accountant to validate the correctness of the share that the Municipality is receiving from the Province in accordance with the authorized rates and the reported volume of quarry extractions.
observations.managementAction: Validation with PENRO was not yet made due to pandemic.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the person liable to settle her disallowances within the prescribed period pursuant to Section 7.1.1 of the Rules and Regulations on Settlement of Accounts (RRSA).

We further recommended that the LDRRM Officer keep all the LDRRM supplies and equipment in the LDRRM Office and in a more accessible storage room for easy retrieval during emergency cases and to safeguard it against loss and wastage through illegal or improper use or disposition.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other personnel concerned enforce the conversion of the controlled dumpsite and develop and create a sanitary landfill as a final disposal site to ensure protection of public health and environment.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Designated MENRO to submit the Solid Waste Management Plan and Budget and accomplishment report for CY 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the concerned PHW refund the overpayment of hazard pay amounting ₱28,411.80 for the CY 2016, and that the Municipal Accountant comply with the prescribed guidelines under RA 7305 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 for the payment of hazard pay.

Not Implemented

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Solid Waste Board formulate and adopt  other revenue generating measures, such as collecting garbage fees not only from commercial establishments but also from residential  and institutional waste generators, pursuant to Section 12 (4) of Republic Act 9003 in order to ensure the viability of its 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan. 



We recommended that the Local Chief Executive implement the provisions of RA No. 9003 especially on the proper segregation of wastes at source so as to promote recycling and re-use of resources and to reduce the volume of waste for collection and disposal as well as not to expose the constituents to the risk of environmental and health hazards.
observations.managementAction:
Not Implemented

There was no barangay ordinance for the collection of garbage fee that would result to a sharing scheme of the generated income; moreover, the municipality claimed that it has no capacity to collect garbage on a house to house basis.

Not Implemented.

Ocular inspection revealed that garbage cans provided in areas surrounding the municipality were not separated as to biodegradable and non-biodegradable.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management –
instruct the Municipal Treasurer to inform the concerned payees of the existence of these seven (7) stale checks in writing; and
instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw an Adjusting Journal Entry by restoring the cash account and recognizing payable accounts for the equivalent cost of these checks.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly monitor the effectivity and expiration dates of the fidelity bonds of their accountable officers, and cause the renewal of the said fidelity bonds before their expiration in conformity with the provisions of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 and Section 101 of P.D. 1445.

We recommended the management to refrain in allowing and assigning non-regular employees to perform revenue collection pursuant to the afore-said provision of P.D. 1445 in order to safeguard the funds and protect the interest of the government, otherwise, accommodate the said Job Order personnel as a regular employee subject to existing laws, rules and regulations in order for the said personnel to be eligible in securing the Fidelity Bond requirement of the said law.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer cause the submission of the required Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF) monthly, to the Office of the Auditor and strictly comply with the requirements provided by the afore-cited regulation in order to properly facilitate the inventory and monitoring of the accountable forms.

We recommended that the Accounting Office  post its daily transactions and update its subsidiary records to facilitate the determination of balances of every account in the general ledger. More so, a sound internal control requires that balances of accounts should be monitored by the Accountable Officers and by the Accounting Office as well.

We recommended that Management continue on issuing three (3) demand letters to the delinquent borrowers or ensure the refund of the seed money including all accruing interests from CY 1998 to date.  Had there be no compliance on the part of the latter, we further recommended that management take legal action against these defaulting borrowers, if warranted.  Likewise, succeeding Livelihood Loan Assistance Programs should be properly monitored and implemented in accordance to its terms of reference.
We further recommended the Accounting Office to exhaust all possible means to fully account the balance of ₽548,533.92.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management stop paying Transportation Allowance (TA) to officials/employees with assigned government motor vehicles pursuant to Item 8.2.1 of DBM Local Budget Circular No. 103 dated May 15, 2013. Further, it is required that the amount of ₽121,200.00 representing TA granted to these officials from November 2019 to December 2020 be refunded without prejudice to the same allowance granted in the succeeding period, if any.

We reiterated our recommendations that the management prepare/submit:
Monthly Report of Official Travels for every motor vehicle, duly prepared by the Driver concerned summarizing in chronological order his trips for the month.
Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption for every government motor transportation containing among others, the different types of motor vehicles utilized by the LGU during the month, total distance traveled, total fuel used and the normal fuel consumption for each vehicle.
Management Comment: The Audit Team was not provided with comment.

We recommended management to provide explanation on the four deficiencies noted in audit as basis in validating completeness, authenticity and regularity of Emergency Subsidy Program of the government in compliance with the above-stated laws and rules.
We recommended that the required documents be submitted to support the completeness, validity and authenticity of the payment.

We recommended that management submit the following-
accomplished general intake sheet of the 3,333 beneficiaries;
copy of the certificate of eligibility; and
photographs of the beneficiaries during actual receipt of the assistance.
We recommended management to provide full accounting of these items including proof of issuances/distribution to the beneficiaries including inventory of the remaining undistributed items, if any.

We recommended that management utilize the Municipality’s share from Burley and Native Tobacco Excise Tax to those specified programs and projects, thereof. Hence, management should provide explanation on their departure from the existing provisions in the utilization of their CY 2020 tobacco excise tax fund for generator set.

We recommended that Management cause the immediate submission of the five documents/information for proper evaluation and to preclude the issuance of Notice of Suspension.  We further recommended the BAC to determine the costs of goods based on existing market prices which will serve as ceiling in the preparation of the Annual Procurement Plan (APP) as well as in the Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC) prior to posting in the PhilGeps website to avoid incurring similar problems in the future.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that management:
Require the MSWDO, being the principal focal person of this project, to render a full accounting of the ₽2,000,000.00 that was extended to the beneficiaries and names of the accountable officers who accepted remittances and show proof of remittance to the LGU account and return the funds borrowed to continue the project;
Provide control measures that will prohibit those in charge of the project from manipulating the funds of the beneficiaries; and
Conduct the investigation, if warranted, file the appropriate charges against those responsible for the noted irregularities.

We recommended that the MSWDO properly monitor the implementation and distribution of the Social Pension to the senior citizen beneficiaries and ensure delivery of the same within seven (7) to ten (10) days from date of receipt of the fund transferred.

We recommended management to:
Strictly comply with the Government Procurement Law, RA 9184 and its IRR, Guidelines on the Release and Utilization of the Local Government Support Fund – Other Financial Assistance to Local Government Units for the efficient, effective and transparent implementation of the program;
Immediately submit the four (4) enumerated lacking documents to preclude suspension in audit of the transactions; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to refrain from processing vouchers with incomplete documentation.

We recommended that Management ensure completeness of documentation in all government transactions.  We further recommended for the submission of the thirteen (13) previously enumerated documents as basis for technical review.

We recommended Management to strictly observe prudence in the use of government resources and further encourage adopting the “pakyaw system,” in the installation of these street lights for a more transparent and economical utilization of government resources.

We recommended that Management require the Office of the Municipal Engineer to fully account the 54,014 bags of Portland cement as well as the BAC to comply with the pertinent provisions of RA 9184 and its IRR.  Further require said Offices to submit a duly filled-up As built-plans, Statement of Work Accomplished; Program of Works showing the specific project description and location as basis in evaluating actual utilization of said bags of cement as well as in the conduct of technical evaluation.   We further recommended that proper coordination with the barangays as end-users particularly in the planning stage be undertaken before a procurement has to be finalized to avoid wastage of government resources.

We recommended Management:
To provide a written explanation on the non-deduction of 1% retention as   warranty of these procured goods; and
To strictly comply with the provisions of RA 9184 to deduct 1% retention from the payments of goods to the suppliers, and ensure that conditions set forth by the law are observed.

We recommended Management to:
require the Engineering Office, the BAC and Accounting Office to submit the duly approved plans and drawings of the thirteen (13) projects specified under Annex A as well as the necessary supporting documentsmentioned under Annex B; and
instruct the Accounting Office to ensure the completeness of supporting documents of transactions in accordance with the provisions of Section 4 (5) and (6) of P.D. No. 1445 and COA Circular 2012-001 prior to disbursement of funds.

We recommended that management in coordination with the Municipal Health Officer (MHO) be required to submit the two (2) stated documents as basis in evaluating the LGU’s compliance with the program and its impact.

We recommended the LGU Cabatuan, through the MPMIU, to closely monitor and supervise the activities of the farm equipment as well as inform the recipient cooperative to submit the complete financial reports regularly as required in the said Agreement.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Solid Waste Management Board to revisit the Municipality’s Solid Waste Management (SWM) Plan and implement the same in accordance with Republic Act No. (RA) 9003.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other personnel concerned to enforce the closure of the open dumpsite and develop and create a sanitary landfill as a final disposal site to ensure protection of public health and environment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the officials and employees concerned comply with the provisions of the Philippine Commission on Women (PCW), Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG), Department of Budget and Management (DBM) and National Economic and Development Authority (NEDA) Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01 re: Guidelines on the Localization of the Magna Carta of Women, as amended by Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2016-01. 

We further recommended that the officials concerned submit, together with the GAD Accomplishment Report, the documents enumerated above to determine the appropriate amount of the programs/projects/activities to be attributed to GAD budget.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee to furnish the Auditor with copies of all perfected contracts, purchase orders and the like, and all supporting documents forming part thereof for reference or incorporation therein within five (5) working days from the execution of the contracts and issuance of purchase orders in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001, so that review, inspection and evaluation could be made accordingly.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to exert effort in the collection of tax delinquencies through publication, demand letters and massive tax campaigns in accordance with Sections 254, 255 and 256 of Republic Act No. 7160. 

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to avail of the civil remedies for the collection of real property tax, if warranted by circumstances.

We recommended that the concerned PHW refund the overpayment of hazard pay amounting ₱28,411.80 for the CY 2016, and that the Municipal Accountant comply with the prescribed guidelines under RA 7305 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 for the payment of hazard pay.

We recommended that the Municipal Solid Waste Board formulate and adopt  other revenue generating measures, such as collecting garbage fees not only from commercial establishments but also from residential  and institutional waste generators, pursuant to Section 12 (4) of Republic Act 9003 in order to ensure the viability of its 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan. 




We recommended that the Local Chief Executive implement the provisions of RA No. 9003 especially on the proper segregation of wastes at source so as to promote recycling and re-use of resources and to reduce the volume of waste for collection and disposal as well as not to expose the constituents to the risk of environmental and health hazards.

We recommended that Management continue on issuing three (3) demand letters to the delinquent borrowers or ensure the refund of the seed money including all accruing interest from CY 1998 to date.  Had there be no compliance on the part of the latter, we further recommended that management take legal action against these defaulting borrowers, if warranted.
Likewise, succeeding Livelihood Loan Assistance Programs should be properly monitored and implemented in accordance to its terms of reference.

We recommended that Management strictly observe completeness of documents including filling up the required information on all transactions prior to payment.  Comply with the documentary requirements prescribed under P.D. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.
Also, please submit receipt evidencing payment made to Registry of Deeds relative to cost of lot registration in the amount of ₽49,068.51.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant fully disclose the extent of compliance of the LGU in terms of accounting and reporting guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2015-008 on Local Road Networks.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant extensively evaluate the validity of the said unsupported account balance in the amount of ₽141,579.14.  Determine the existence of contract, as well as determine if no actual claim, administrative or judicial has been filed then effect the necessary reversion of the account, if warranted.

We recommended that Management stop paying Transportation Allowance (TA) to officials with assigned government motor vehicles pursuant to Item 8.2.1 of DBM Local Budget Circular No. 103 dated May 15, 2013. Further, it is required that the amount of ₱14,400.00 and ₱13,600.00 or a total of ₱28,000.00 representing TA granted to the Municipal Mayor and Municipal Vice Mayor, respectively from November 2019 to December 2019 be refunded without prejudice to the same allowance granted for the months of January 2020 and succeeding period, if any.

We recommended management to:
Submit Monthly Report of Official Travels for every motor vehicle, duly prepared by the Driver concerned summarizing in chronological order his trips for the month.
Submit Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption for every government motor transportation containing among others, the different types of motor vehicles utilized by the LGU during the month, total distance traveled, total fuel used and the normal fuel consumption for each vehicle.
Ensure that the trip tickets are properly accomplished and duly approved by authorized officials before embarking on a trip.

We recommended management to:
Maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund by speeding up the implementation of the prioritized programs, projects and activities funded out of it as per Annual Investment Plan and that closer coordination among the officials involved in planning, execution and monitoring be undertaken to achieve the desired level of outcomes.
Require the Local Finance Committee to conduct semi-annual and general examination of cost and accomplishments against performance standards in undertaking development projects, as provided under Section 316(h) of RA 7160.
Instruct the Municipal Planning Development Officer to immediately look into the unimplemented developmental projects as embodied in the Annual Investment Plan.  Identify causes of non-implementation and develop feasible and specific solutions to effectively implement developmental projects.
Instruct the Municipal Engineer to be prudent and precise in evaluating the timely and proper implementation of each and every project.  Likewise, he should also conduct regular inspection of the projects so that accomplishment is monitored and evaluated based on the project implementation schedule as stated in the Annual Investment Plan.

We recommended that management thru the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office to fully implement the planned programs, activities and projects for disaster preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery so that the intended benefits could be fully achieved.

We recommended that management immediately submit these lacking documents specified in the attached schedule to facilitate the conduct of review and technical evaluation of these infrastructure contracts.

We recommended that management submit/furnish copies of contracts and Purchase Orders within five (5) days upon issuance and Notices of Delivery and Inspection and Acceptance Reports within twenty-four (24) hours from receipt of delivery to the Audit Team for review.  Likewise, strictly observe the provision of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular No. 96-010 on the submission of all future contracts and its supporting documents including copies of notices of deliveries, inspection and acceptance within the stated period.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the LGU conduct monitoring and evaluation on the beneficiaries’ business operation with the end view of providing guidance towards successful implementation of their livelihood program.  Likewise, require the beneficiaries who are no longer interested to continue the program to return to LGU the food cart including seed capital pursuant to the provision of the MOA.  Also, MOA is required to be renewed yearly pursuant to the terms and conditions of the Agreement.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that management:
Require the MSWDO, being the principal focal person of this project, to render a full accounting of the ₽2,000,000.00 that was extended to the beneficiaries and names of the accountable officers who accepted remittances and show proof of remittance to the LGU account;
Send demand letter or regular statement of account to Ms. Mones and other accountable officers who accepted collections but did not remit to the LGU account; and
Initiate legal action to collect these receivables from officers/employees and individuals who are no longer connected with the LGU, if warranted.

We commended management for their continuous effort in implementing these projects however, the noted deficiencies that needs to be corrected be undertaken.  Also, the required documents such as the Final Statement of Work Accomplished” (Final SWA) be submitted to the Audit Team.  Moreover, other noted deficiencies be refunded to the coffers of the LGU.

We recommended that management in coordination with the Provincial Environment Monitoring Board of Isabela and concerned barangay officials to:
monitor quarry operations in their respective barangays/localities;
report to the concerned authorities (Provincial Management Monitoring Board) all quarry operators who are extracting quarry materials beyond the volume stated in the hauling slip and outside of the allowed quarry sites; and
require the municipal accountant to validate the correctness of the share that the Municipality is receiving from the Province in accordance with the authorized rates and the reported volume of quarry extractions.

We recommended Management to:
Coordinate with the local sanggunian to ratify the imposition of penalties for delinquent water system concessionaires;
direct the responsible Office to immediately send demand letters to delinquent concessionaires to pay their arrears; and
strictly enforce the provisions embodied in the contract agreement particularly the disconnection of water service to delinquent concessionaires with account not paid five (5) days after due date.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to send demand letters to delinquent taxpayers and conduct information campaign, to all real property owners within the Municipality, with emphasis of granting discounts when paid before due dates and the possibility of losing their property for not paying at all.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that management in coordination with the local sanggunian to:
Execute lease contract agreement to all qualified occupants; and
Update the inventory of market stalls and provide all the necessary information to facilitate computation of exact rental to be collected, therefrom.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the person liable to settle her disallowances within the prescribed period pursuant to Section 7.1.1 of the Rules and Regulations on Settlement of Accounts (RRSA).

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the Inventory Committee to cause the reconciliation of the results of actual physical count with the property and accounting records pursuant to the pertinent provisions of the Manual on NGAS for LGU and Government Accounting Manual (GAAM). The discrepancy between the Report on Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) and the accounting records should be investigated, reconciled and adjusted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor see to it that the provisions of DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2017-1 on the use of the 20% Development Fund is properly observed so that appropriate utilization and implementation of the developmental projects is carried out effectively and efficiently.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the designated Inventory and Disposal Committee Chairman and the LDRRM Officer to conduct regular inventory of LDRRM supplies and equipment available at its disposal and to update and reconcile its records to aid the concerned officials of the Municipality in its decision-making process in conformity with Section VI of COA Circular No. 2014-002.
We further recommended that the LDRRM Officer keep all the LDRRM supplies and equipment in the LDRRM Office and in a more accessible storage room for easy retrieval during emergency cases and to safeguard it against loss and wastage through illegal or improper use or disposition.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board (MSWMB) to revisit the Municipality’s Solid Waste Management Plan and implement the same in accordance with Republic Act No. (RA) 9003.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other personnel concerned enforce the conversion of the controlled dumpsite and develop and create a sanitary landfill as a final disposal site to ensure protection of public health and environment.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Designated MENRO to submit the Solid Waste Management Plan and Budget and accomplishment report for CY 2019.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee to stop the practice of splitting of contracts and strictly adhere with the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 in all of its procurement activity.
We further recommended that the officials concerned meticulously and judiciously plan the procurement by preparing the Annual Procurement Plan (APP) in compliance to R.A. No 9184.
We also recommended the submission of the required documents enumerated above so that the subject transactions will not be suspended in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee to furnish the Auditor with copies of all perfected contracts, purchase orders and the like, and all supporting documents forming part thereof for reference or incorporation therein within five (5) working days from the execution of the contracts and issuance of purchase orders in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to exert effort in the collection of tax delinquencies through publication, demand letters and massive tax campaigns in accordance with Sections 254, 255 and 256 of Republic Act No. 7160.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to avail of the civil remedies for the collection of real property tax, if warranted by circumstances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to regularly remit the amounts due to GSIS within the required remittance period to avoid penalties and surcharges pursuant to Section 6(b) of R.A. 8291.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the records to facilitate remittance of prior years’ balances and provide a detailed breakdown of the balances of each trust liabilities as of December 31, 2019 so as to identify the period and names of personnel these balances are attributed to.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all other officials concerned observe the schedule for GAD Planning and Budgeting as provided for by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01, as amended by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2016-01 dated January 12, 2016 to facilitate the timely submission to the Provincial Planning and Development Office and the subsequent review and endorsement by the DILG to the PCW.

We recommended the following:

[a] Conduct an inventory of all Public Infrastructure assets under the LGU’s jurisdiction using the format prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-008.  Ensure that all the fields in the form be properly filled up and furnish the Audit Team a copy as basis in validating the correctness of such accounts;

[b] Determine all Public Infrastructure assets transferred to RPI from CY 2014 backwards which are not yet fully depreciated or impaired to be restored and recorded in the books as part of PPE accounts; and

[c] Ensure compliance with accounting and reporting guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2015-008 by directing the key officials to perform the duties and responsibilities assigned to them.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Inventory Committee to review the Report of the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) and settle unreconciled accounts as well as identify all unserviceable properties and equipment and proper disposal be made within reasonable time in accordance with Section 79 of PD No. 1445.

We also recommended that the Inventory Committee make effort to identify the remaining unascertained properties in order to establish the correctness of the PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and all officials and employees concerned comply with the provisions of COA Circular 97-002, as failure of the Accountable Officer to liquidate his cash advance within the prescribed period shall constitute for the withholding of his salary and the instruction of other sanctions. 

We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to make adjustments so as to correct the erroneous recording of the loss laptop and cash shortage in the cash advance account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to deposit the full amount of collections not later than the next banking day to the authorized depository bank and to strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 69 of PD 1445, Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382 and Section 32 of MNGAS for LGU, Vol. 1.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: 

a. Prepare and submit monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements within the reglementary period; in order to check the accuracy of the balances of the bank accounts and to submit the journal entry vouchers to ensure proper recording of all valid reconciling items that require adjustment/correction in the GL. 


b. Make a formal request to the depository bank for the release of bank statements with corresponding debit/credit advices within the prescribed regulations.
c.	Conduct periodic reconciliation with the Municipal Treasurer of the Cash in Bank balances per books and per cashbooks.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor see to it that the provisions of DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2017-1 on the use of 20% Development Fund be properly observed so that appropriate utilization and implementation of the developmental projects is carried out effectively and efficiently and to discontinue from charging similar expenses that have no capital expenditure property or components to avoid sanctions such as possible disallowance. 

We likewise recommended that projects/programs with general use or having administrative nature must be appropriated/funded out of the General Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MDRRMO to prioritize the use of LDRRMF and prepare the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan (LDRRMP) in accordance with laws, rules and regulations to support disaster mitigation, preparation, response, rehabilitation and recovery.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the MDRRM Officer to prepare and submit each month a Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF to the audit team on or before the 15th day after the end of each month, through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council and the Municipal Development Council. Provisions of Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular 2012-002 should be strictly followed

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the employees concerned to strictly comply with the provisions of R.A. 9184 and its implementing rules and regulations on the procurement of goods and services.

We likewise recommended that payments for supplies and materials as well as other operating expenses be made directly to suppliers in order to properly deduct/withhold the mandatory taxes due to the national government.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to regularly remit the amounts due to the BIR, GSIS, and Philhealth within the required remittance period to avoid penalties and surcharges pursuant to Section 69 of PD 1445, Section 6(b) of RA 8291, and RA 7875 as amended by RA 9241.

We further recommended that collections from the sources identified in item 6.1 of DBM Budget Circular No. 2004-5A to be used exclusively for the payment of honoraria and overtime pay of BAC and TWG members be transferred in the Trust Fund books.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to implement the proportionate reduction from the Transportation Allowance (TA) of employees and officials who were issued/assigned motor vehicles.

We also recommended that payments for Transportation Allowance should be in accordance with DBM Local Budget Circular No. 102, Section 55 of the General Provisions of GAA of FY 2018 and COA Circular. 2000-005, to avoid suspension and disallowance of the same.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require all department heads to submit at the beginning of the year, the list of all on-going government projects/programs/activities and those that are to be implemented during the year together with the Consolidated Quarterly Report on Government Projects/ Programs/Activities for the quarter ending March 31 to December 31, 2018 and years thereafter to ensure compliance with laws, rules and regulations particularly COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013.

infrastructure project or before the start of the program/project/activity that the appropriate signboards and/or public notices are already posted for validation.

books of accounts so as to facilitate submission thereof to the auditor within the first ten (10) days of the ensuing month.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive  direct the Disposal Committee to follow  the procedures and guidelines on the disposal as well as the forms to be used in reporting thereof.

observations.managementAction:
As of today, partial compliance with the requirements mentioned under paragraph 6.5 thru submission of copy of DTI certificate and proof of ownership over the equipment such as the OR/CR including their comments dated June 25, 2018 to our audit observations and recommendations, particularly item No. 6 thereof wherein they abreast the Audit Team on their actions taken thereon such as:

Audit observation under 6.4 (b), management informed the Audit Team that:

“The supporting documents namely, Canvass Papers, Abstract of Bids, Purchase Request, Purchase Order, Deed of Absolute Sale, Inspection and Acceptance Report will be dated accordingly.”

To date, the Audit Team was not furnished with these stated duly accomplished documents. 

As to observation under 6.4 (c) they also explained that:

“The procurement of one (1) unit reconditioned 8-wheeler fire truck was already included in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) of the Municipality for CY 2014.”

In our evaluation, although Municipal Ordinance No. 2017-13 was enacted to finance the LGU share in the acquisition of fire truck and despite its inclusion in the Annual Supplemental Procurement Plan for CY 2017, the unit should have been included in CY 2017 Annual Investment Plan considering that the appropriation for capital outlay if not utilized, will stand only for two (2) year period or until CY 2016.

As to observation under 6.4 (d), management justified that:

“The useful life of the equipment was re-assessed.  As a result, it was found out that this is still useful and in good running condition and no record of repair had been accounted after its acquisition.  Hence, the 20-year acquisition period is immaterial and was not considered.”

Scrutiny of records disclosed that a total of ₱47,040.00 were utilized for repairs and maintenance of said equipment for the period June 16, 2017 until March 16, 2018.

However, based on subsidiary records there were maintenance expenses already incurred prior to its purchase totaling ₱68,740.00, drawing confusion why these expenditures incurred considering that the said fire truck was just acquired on June 16, 2017.  Hence, these constitute irregular and unnecessary charges against public funds as defined under COA Circular 2012-003, in line with Section 33 of P.D. No. 1445.  A Notice of Disallowance will be issued upon securing the pertinent vouchers from the storage room.

Recommendation
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare and submit monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements within the reglementary period; in order to check the accuracy of the balances of the bank accounts and to submit the journal entry vouchers to ensure proper recording of all valid reconciling items that require adjustment/correction in the GL.
Make a formal request to the depository bank for the release of bank statements with corresponding debit/credit advices within the prescribed regulations.
Conduct periodic reconciliation with the Municipal Treasurer of the Cash in Bank balances per books and per cashbooks.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2017 -002 dated February 14, 2017.
Management Comment
Management, in its letter dated March 13, 2017, informed that the reason why the bank reconciliation statements were submitted in lump is because the official bank statements were issued to them in lump containing several months.  Due to such delay, the management had to rely on snapshots in the preparation of their December bank reconciliation.

Recommendations
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer send a written notice to the payees or holder in due course to surrender the stale check for cancellation and issuance of a new one.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant make adjusting entry for stale checks that remained outstanding and cannot be presented for payment to determine the correct balances of Cash in Bank – Local Currency and Accounts Payable account.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2017 -008 dated February 14, 2017.
Management Comment
Management, in a letter dated March 13, 2017, informed that the Municipal Accountant will trace the nature of these checks and will make necessary adjusting entries to correct the same.

Recommendation
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer   see to it that collections are deposited intact and within the reglementary period pursuant to the provisions of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs) and the Revised Cash Examination Manual to avoid possible loss or misuse of government funds.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2017 -011 dated February 14, 2017.
Management Comment
Management, in a letter dated March 13, 2017, informed that they will comply with the Recommendation as soon as possible. Authority from the Sangguniang Bayan will be requested for the opening of new checking accounts with the Land Bank Philippines – Janiuay Branch in order to deposit daily.
Delay in the deposit was due to delayed preparations of reports of collectors, reconciliation of the cashier with collectors and official receipts versus abstract of collections.

Recommendation
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Petty Cash Custodian that only non-recurring emergency and petty operating expenses will be charged against the fund and not the regular ones.  Likewise, adherence to Section 48 of MNGAS for LGU and Section 4.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 is hereby enjoined.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2017 -010 dated February 14, 2017.
Management Comment
Management, in its letter dated March 13, 2017, informed that the transaction should have been named Regular Cash Advance to pay approved disbursement vouchers of recurring aids in crisis situation, and reimbursements of small amounts. The intention was to refrain from preparing checks and pay or reimburse the payees for they need also the money.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The Audit Team still recommends that the Municipality refrain from charging the abovementioned charges against petty cash fund and should adhere to the guidelines provided for in Section 48 of MNGAS for LGU and Section 4.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002.

Recommendation
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and all officials and employees concerned comply with the provisions of COA Circular 97-002, as failure of the Accountable Officer to liquidate his cash advance within the prescribed period shall constitute for the withholding of his salary and the instruction of other sanctions. The Municipal Accountant should also furnish the Local Chief Executive as well the department heads and division chiefs, a listing of long-outstanding unliquidated cash advances of employees under their charge to enforce liquidation / settlement.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2017 -003 dated February 14, 2017.
Management Comment
Management, in a letter dated March 13, 2017, informed that out of the P445,126.88, P300,000.00 consist of advances for confidential expenses, liquidation of which was duly mailed to the Intelligence and Confidential Unit of the COA Central office, waiting for credit notice that will warrant its liquidation in the municipality’s books of accounts. P2,392.00 was duly refunded in January 5, 2017 and the rest are being refunded in installment basis.

Recommendation
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all officials and employees that provisions of COA Circular 97-002 shall be strictly complied with. The Municipality should refrain from charging cash advances which are confidential in nature against the Other Maintenance and Operating Expense Account, instead should be charged to the proper account such as the Confidential Fund and Intelligence Fund.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2017 -007 dated February 14, 2017.
Management Comment
Management, in a letter dated March 13, 2017, informed that due to the delay in the issuance of credit notices from the Intelligence Auditing Unit of COA Main Office, the Municipality stopped its practice of granting cash advances for confidential expenses and instead granted it as a regular cash advance. But due to the confidential nature of the expense, the utilization of the marked money, including its payees, is privy to the Chief of Police alone, thus the total amount was granted to him. Meanwhile, the Municipality will cease granting cash advances for the said purpose.

Recommendation
We recommended that the Disposal Committee be guided by the procedures and guidelines on the disposal as well as the forms to be used in reporting thereof.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant adhere to the proper accounting of unserviceable properties pursuant to Section 140 of the MNGAS for LGU, Volume III, to ensure correct presentation of accounts in the financial statements.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2017 -013 dated February 14, 2017.
Management Comment
Management informed that they will comply with the abovementioned findings and will refer such to the Disposal Committee for appropriate action. Some of the vehicles had already been inspected by the Technical Audit Specialist Team but no report was provided, hence the delay in the disposal.

Recommendations
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive revisit Section 447 Paragraph 5 (xi) of RA 7160 and shall refrain from incurring additional expenses contrary to the provisions of the law.
We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive communicate with the Provincial Government of Iloilo to accommodate the municipal scholars in their scholarship programs so as not to impair the privilege that has been given to the students upon the grant of the said scholarship.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2017 -017 dated February 14, 2017.
Management Comment
Management informed that the scholarship program has been around for a while and it would be difficult to suddenly terminate the said practice. Moreover, the scholarships provided for by the Provincial Government have high standards; hence scholars of the municipality would not be easily admitted to their program.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The Audit Team understands the noble purpose of the Municipality in granting scholarships to underprivileged constituents, however, there are existing guidelines which laws provide in granting said programs that we should adhere to. Hence, the Team maintains its recommendation that the Municipality should refrain from incurring additional expenses contrary to the provisions of the law.

Recommendation
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and concerned officials refrain from charging expenditures for maintenance and other operating expenses on the 20% MDF and instead, appropriate such expenses in the MOOE of the municipality. In addition, the municipality should see to it that the 20% MDF will be used to finance priority development projects and programs that partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2017 -014 dated February 14, 2017.
Management Comment
Management informed that some of the charges were indeed in the nature of repairs and maintenance hence should be properly charged against the Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses Account. The management further added that they will refrain from charging expenses of such nature against the Municipal Development Fund.

Recommendations
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the concerned personnel (Project in Charge, Department Heads, BAC and BAC Secretariat) to strictly observe Rule II, Section 7 of RA 9184 and COA Circular No. 76-41 as well as the imposition of control measures to ensure that procurement is effected in an efficient, economical and effective manner that is most advantageous to the government and to prevent audit disallowances for such violation.
We further recommended strict compliance to the provisions of RA 9184 otherwise proper sanctions be imposed on future similar transactions.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2017 -009 dated February 14, 2017.
Management Comment
Management informed that some of the transactions resulted from the failure of the Provincial government to deliver the jetmatic pumps that they committed to provide; hence the agency purchased the items itself. Public bidding was not conducted for the items were for the constituents affected by El Niño.

Recommendation
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Bids and Awards Committee to instruct BAC Secretariat to post all procurement in the Portal as prescribed in the above-mentioned laws, rules and regulations to promote transparency and achieve efficiency in the procurement process of the municipality.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2017 -012 dated February 14, 2017.
Management Comment
Management informed that the Bids and Awards Committee only commenced to post procurements using the alternative mode in January 2017.

Recommendations
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive  through the Sangguniang Bayan hire qualified personnel to occupy the three (3) Staff positions to assist the LDRRMO following the position titles and salary grades set by Rule 6 Section 2 of IRR of RA No. 10121 and Item 5.2, 5.3 and 5.4 of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014 to be able to properly perform their functions and responsibilities as LDRRM Officer and Staff.
We recommended that the LDRRMO adhere strictly with the provisions of Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 so that the proper utilization of the Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund will be verified as to correctness in a timely manner by the auditor.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2017 -005 dated February 14, 2017.
Management Comment
Management informed that there is already a plan for reorganization, designating a personnel who could focus on  the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office. It was also added that staff positions are to be filled by job hired personnel. However, the management finds it impracticable to follow the salary grades that the circular has provided.

Recommendations
We recommended that the Municipal Administrator/Municipal Engineer:
Submit regularly to the Auditor the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption for verification purposes to determine the reasonableness of fuel and lubricants consumed during the period and to effectively control fuel, gasoline and oil expenditures and avoid wasteful, excessive and unnecessary expenditures for fuel consumption.
Exercise proper control over the use of government vehicles and fuel consuming equipment through properly accomplished and duly approved, serially numbered Driver’s Trip Tickets, a summary of which shall be made at the end of each month in the Monthly Report of Official Travels for audit purposes.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2017 -006 dated February 14, 2017.
Management Comment
Management, in a letter dated March 13, 2017, informed that the official travel form was referred to the Engineer’s Office c/o the person in-charge of the motor vehicles of the Municipality.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The Audit Team still recommends that the Local Chief Executive designate an officer in the Engineering Office whose priority is to focus on the up keeping of documents related to Fuel Consumption and Official Travels.

Recommendation
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer see to it that disbursement vouchers, payrolls and its supporting documents are conspicuously cancelled/invalidated as “PAID” after each payment to preclude double payment.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2017 -001 dated February 14, 2017.
Management Comment
Management, in a letter dated March 13, 2017, informed that they will comply with the abovementioned recommendation and will cancel/invalidate disbursement vouchers and other supporting documents by stamping “PAID.”

Recommendations
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive shall:
1.   	Reduce the number of job hires and contractual personnel especially those performing clerical and administrative functions.  Hiring of personnel shall be made as actual need arises and be deployed only to offices that needed their services the most.
2. 	Require the rendition of overtime services by regular employees in the exigency of the service and to meet reports on deadlines.
3.   	Adopt merit and incentive award strategies to motivate all personnel and to enhance their potentials in performing their functions.
4.  Regular job rotation should be made to place the right person on the right job to deliver the services required.
We further recommended that the agency head shall revisit the guidelines set forth in CSC MC No. 24, s. 2002 on the hiring of job hires and contractual personnel. Judicious use of government funds must be considered so that savings derived could be used on developmental projects most beneficial in uplifting the lives of its constituents.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2017 -015 dated February 14, 2017.
Management Comment
Management informed that the Local Chief Executive personally assesses the need to hire job order personnel by requiring the requesting department to submit reports to justify such need. Hence, the Local Chief Executive asserts that every job order personnel hired by the Municipality plays an essential role in the Municipality’s operation. In addition, the Management in a letter dated March 22, 2017, informed that there were only 22 job orders performing clerical and administrative functions, the rest was assigned to various project based activities in the municipality.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
In spite the explanation of the Management, the Audit Team still recommends that the agency reduce the number of job hired personnel to its minimum for it was determined upon inspection that the various offices of the Municipality had job hired personnel performing clerical functions.

Recommendation
We recommended that the concerned PHW refund the overpayment of hazard pay amounting P28,411.80 for the CY 2016, and that the Municipal Accountant comply with the prescribed guidelines under RA 7305 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 for the payment of hazard pay.
Management Comment
The Management informed that they will observe the prescribed guidelines under RA 7305 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 for the payment of hazard pay, thus will refrain from granting the fixed amount of P4,989.75 per month for hazard pay.

Recommendations
We recommended that the Municipal Solid Waste Board formulate and adopt  other revenue generating measures, such as collecting garbage fees not only from commercial establishments but also from residential  and institutional waste generators, pursuant to Section 12 (4) of Republic Act 9003 in order to ensure the viability of its 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan.We also recommended that the Municipal Solid Waste Board review and update the Municipality’s 10-year Solid Waste Plan pursuant to Section 16 of Republic Act 9003.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive implement the provisions of RA No. 9003 especially on the proper segregation of wastes at source so as to promote recycling and re-use of resources and to reduce the volume of waste for collection and disposal as well as not to expose the constituents to the risk of environmental and health hazards.
Management Comment
Management informed that they are planning to re establish the MRFs in the Barangay Levels so as to promote segregation at source. Furthermore, the Local Chief Executive expressed his intention to assign three personnel from the Engineering  Department to focus on matters concerning Solid Waste Management.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer stop the practice of drawing cash advances and transfer the same to another accountable officer. Municipal Officials shall observe efficiency and economy in spending Municipal funds and see to it that the incurrence of expenditures shall be dictated by the demands of good government, and that the mission and thrusts of the agency incurring the expenditure must be considered in determining whether or not the expenditure is necessary. The call for austerity measures as prescribed in DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2007-83, dated July 25, 2007, be strictly followed.
We also recommended the immediate refund of P196,835.90 paid to Municipal Officials and employees concerned.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace up the unreconciled balance of Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts and make necessary adjustment, where appropriate, to reflect the correct account balances and present reliable data on the Municipality’s financial operation as of given period of time.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive take the following courses of action:
Direct the Inventory Committee to complete the physical count of the remaining Property, Plant and Equipment valued in the LGU’s books of accounts totaling P126,106,929.57 in consonance with Section 124 of Manual on the New Government Accounting System for Local Government Units, Volume I.
b. 	 We Recommended that the Treasurer’s Office, in the absence of the heads of the   General Services Office and the Accounting Office, shall assign qualified personnel to maintain separate ledger cards for all property and to work on the reconciliation of the accounting and property records with the Report on Physical Count of PPE in order to establish correctness of the PPE accounts.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Sangguniang Bayan revisit Section 325(a) of Republic Act No. 7160 and Executive Order No. 181 and shall refrain from incurring additional PS expenses/cost until such time that the limitation is observed. The implementation of any increase in PS cost shall be held in abeyance until the municipality shall not exceed PS limitation/cap.
We further recommended that the concerned Municipal Officials and Employees cause the refund of the 2015 PEI amounting to P2,262,654.50.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive observe efficiency and economy in spending Municipal funds and see to it that the incurrence of expenditures should be dictated by the demands of good government, and that the mission and thrusts of the agency incurring the expenditure must be considered in determining whether or not the expenditure is necessary. The call for austerity measures as prescribed in AO 103 dated August 31, 2004, is likewise recommended to be strictly followed.
Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer refrain from paying traveling expenses without authority from the DILG Director and direct attention to the provisions of DILG MC No. 2011-59 dated April 25, 2011.
We also recommended that the concerned municipal officials and employees cause the refund of the total amount of P293,440.00 representing the traveling expenses incurred on Lakbay Aral.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and LDRRM Officer adhere strictly with the pertinent provisions of RA No. 10121 and National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council, Department of Budget and Management and Department of Interior and Local Government Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 to preclude disallowances.
We recommended that all transfers/advances shall be recognized as Receivables in the books of GF – Proper of the Municipality and Liability in the books of the CEEDO. Correcting entries shall be made for the transfer of fund amounting to P10,667,329.00 from GF to CEEDO for fair presentation of financial statements, hence, a Journal Entry Voucher must be drawn by the Municipal Accountant.
We also recommended that income generated by the CEEDO shall be applied in accordance with the provisions of Section 313 of RA 7160 and 3.2 of LBC 100.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor together with the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and the Municipal Engineer properly plan and program projects to carry out the purpose for which it was granted.
We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System carefully study PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 and follow the procedural guidelines in the formulation of GAD Plan and Budget and GAD Accomplishment Report.
We likewise recommended that the GAD Focal Point System see to it that GPB is submitted to DILG for review and approval in order that the same will be incorporated in their Annual Budget to be enacted by their Sanggunian.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all Municipal officials observe efficiency and economy in spending Municipal funds.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all Municipal Officials see to it that the incurrence of expenditures should be dictated by the demands of good government, and that the mission and thrusts of the agency incurring the expenditure must be considered in determining whether or not the expenditure is necessary.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to refrain from paying travelling expenses without authority from the DILG Director and direct attention to the provisions of DILG MC No. 2011-59 dated April 25, 2011.
The call for austerity measures as prescribed in AO 103 dated August 31, 2004, is likewise recommended to be strictly followed.
We also recommended that all Municipal Officials and employees cause the refund of the total amount P1,782,000.00 representing the traveling expenses of the 82 regular personnel of the LGU.
We recommended that all Municipal Officials and Employees cause the refund of the CY 2014 Productivity Enhancement Incentive at P10,000.00 each for the 125 employees.
We recommended that the management cause the refund of the 2013 productivity enhancement incentive at P15,000.00 each for the 118 employees and at P10,500.00 each for the 8 employees.
We recommended that management cause the refund of the amount in excess of P5,000.00 for each employee granted under E.O. No. 80.
The Management to require the Municipal Health Officer to refund the overpayment of hazard pay amounting to P38,108.40 which exceeded the prescribed rates of 5% of their monthly basic salary for health workers with salary grade 20 and above pursuant to Section 21 of R.A. 7305.
The Local Chief Executive shall ensure the titling of all parcels of land owned by the Municipality and by prioritizing the appropriation of funds for the purpose of establishing ownership thereof and thus avoid future litigation.
